case_name: Brackish groundwater polishing for recharge with mixed VOC and trace pharmaceutical constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of treatment process chains for brackish groundwater containing ibuprofen, MTBE, and cis-1,2-dichloroethylene intended for groundwater recharge under strict effluent, footprint, and moderate cost constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A coastal inland-basin utility plans to recover impacted brackish groundwater and use the treated water for managed aquifer recharge near drinking-water supply wells. The source contains a mixed contaminant profile: a highly volatile oxygenate (MTBE), a chlorinated solvent daughter product (cis-1,2-dichloroethylene), and a trace pharmaceutical (ibuprofen), while salinity is elevated but not the primary compliance driver. The regulator has imposed very low recharge limits for the organic contaminants and requires a robust process with limited site footprint. Several treatment routes are technically feasible, but the preferred option must balance removal certainty, compactness, operating burden, and residuals handling rather than simply maximizing contaminant rejection.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should reliably achieve very low organic contaminant concentrations suitable for protective groundwater recharge permitting, with strong compliance confidence for MTBE, cis-1,2-dichloroethylene, and ibuprofen.
secondary_goals: minimize treatment footprint on a constrained wellfield site
avoid unnecessary desalination if not needed for permit compliance
maintain manageable O&M for municipal field staff
limit difficult residual or concentrate disposal obligations

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer minimal hazardous residual generation and avoid large brine concentrate streams
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: must maintain compliance despite moderate influent variability and seasonal pumping changes

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_filtration: remove particulates and protect downstream stripping and adsorption equipment
core_unit_functions.packed_tower_aeration: strip volatile contaminants such as MTBE and cis-1,2-dichloroethylene from brackish groundwater
core_unit_functions.vapor_phase_gac_offgas_control: capture stripped volatile organics from air emissions
core_unit_functions.liquid_phase_gac_polishing: polish residual MTBE and cis-1,2-dichloroethylene and adsorb ibuprofen for low final effluent concentrations

applicability_rationale: the contaminant mix contains two compounds with meaningful volatility, making air stripping an efficient bulk-removal step for MTBE and cis-1,2-dichloroethylene
adding liquid-phase GAC addresses the less volatile trace pharmaceutical and provides polishing needed for strict recharge limits
this train is more compact than a full advanced oxidation plus biological polishing system and avoids desalination concentrate management
for brackish groundwater, elevated TDS does not strongly penalize stripping or adsorption performance compared with membrane-based options
the combined train balances strong compliance confidence with moderate cost and manageable municipal O&M
potential_risks: MTBE stripping efficiency can be lower than for more volatile chlorinated solvents, so tower design and polishing capacity must be conservative
spent GAC replacement or reactivation planning is required
air permitting and off-gas control are necessary to avoid transferring contamination to the atmosphere
ibuprofen adsorption performance can decline if DOC increases materially

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_filtration: remove solids and improve UV transmittance reliability
core_unit_functions.uv_hydrogen_peroxide_advanced_oxidation: oxidize MTBE, cis-1,2-dichloroethylene, and ibuprofen and reduce trace organics to lower levels
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_gac: remove oxidation byproducts, provide residual adsorption capacity, and stabilize effluent quality

applicability_rationale: AOP is one of the more technically comprehensive options for this mixed contaminant set, especially when very low recharge limits drive need for aggressive polishing
the process has a compact footprint relative to large biological reactor systems and can address contaminants that are not all equally amenable to a single physical separation step
post-BAC helps manage partially oxidized intermediates and improves compliance robustness
this route becomes attractive where site footprint is critical and the utility can support moderate-to-higher process control requirements
potential_risks: higher energy and chemical demand than stripping plus GAC
oxidation byproduct control and UV transmittance monitoring require more operator attention
halide-containing brackish water can complicate AOP performance and byproduct considerations
insufficient downstream biological or carbon polishing could leave residual transformation products

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_filtration: protect carbon beds from solids loading
core_unit_functions.liquid_phase_gac_lead_lag: adsorb ibuprofen and provide polishing for MTBE and cis-1,2-dichloroethylene with staged breakthrough control

applicability_rationale: lead-lag GAC is operationally straightforward and avoids air emissions and high-pressure concentrate management
the process footprint can be moderate if vessel design is compact and vertical
GAC is particularly reasonable for the trace pharmaceutical and can contribute to VOC polishing
this route may appeal when the utility prioritizes simpler operation over maximum process intensity
potential_risks: MTBE has relatively poor adsorption compared with many other organics, which can cause rapid breakthrough and large carbon usage
cis-1,2-dichloroethylene removal by GAC alone may be less robust for strict low-level recharge targets
high compliance certainty may require frequent monitoring and conservative bed replacement
spent carbon handling remains necessary

core_unit_functions.pretreatment: remove solids and protect downstream units
core_unit_functions.air_stripping: remove the volatile fraction, especially cis-1,2-dichloroethylene and part of MTBE
core_unit_functions.membrane_bioreactor: biodegrade ibuprofen and remaining biodegradable organic fractions while producing a compact clarified effluent

applicability_rationale: this hybrid route can technically address the mixed contaminant profile while maintaining a relatively small hydraulic footprint through membrane separation
biological treatment can be effective for ibuprofen and some residual organics after stripping lowers VOC loading
the configuration may be selected when a utility favors biological polishing over larger carbon consumption
potential_risks: process control is materially more complex than adsorption-only or stripping-plus-polishing systems
biological systems may be less predictable for sustained MTBE polishing unless specifically acclimated and carefully managed
membrane fouling, sludge wasting, and nutrient balance add O&M burden
cold-water or variable loading can reduce biodegradation robustness

core_unit_functions.pretreatment: protect membranes from fouling and scaling
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: remove dissolved salts and reject a broad range of dissolved organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation_post_polish: polish low-molecular-weight organics and provide additional barrier against residual contaminants

applicability_rationale: this route is technically capable and offers broad-spectrum treatment, especially if future salinity limits become more stringent
a compact skid-based layout can fit space-constrained sites
it provides a high-barrier approach when regulators or stakeholders demand highly engineered treatment
potential_risks: brine concentrate management is strongly misaligned with the stated residuals constraint
RO is not the most economical first-choice for this case because salinity is elevated but not the main recharge compliance driver
high-pressure membrane operation, antiscalant management, and cleaning increase operator burden
post-RO AOP may still be required for very low trace-organic targets, increasing complexity further

preferred_rank_pattern: hybrid VOC-focused plus polishing trains should rank near the top
AOP-based treatment should appear as a strong but not necessarily first-ranked compact alternative
GAC-only treatment should not rank first because MTBE and strict limits challenge adsorption-only performance
RO-based treatment should appear as technically capable but downgraded by concentrate and cost misalignment

must_reflect: strict low effluent targets for groundwater recharge require multibarrier reliability rather than single-step optimistic removal claims
high footprint pressure should favor compact trains over large conventional biological systems
moderate cost and residual constraints should penalize full desalination approaches when salinity is not the primary compliance driver

must_contain: why air stripping is relevant for MTBE and cis-1,2-dichloroethylene but insufficient alone for ibuprofen
why AOP can be attractive for mixed trace-organic control under strict limits but carries higher energy and operational burden
why brine concentrate handling causes RO to rank lower despite strong technical capability
why GAC-only treatment is weaker for MTBE-dominant compliance assurance

should_mention: transformation products or byproducts from advanced oxidation
breakthrough or media replacement risk for carbon systems
concentrate disposal burden for membrane systems
